front: Philosopher: argued that virtue, not pleasure, was the end of existence
Philosopher: Diogenes of Sinope was called this thinker's 'faithful hound' for doggedly pursuing him
Work: asserted that there is only one God who is not understandable by humans
Philosopher: the school he founded advocated askesis (discipline) alongside the later Stoics
Philosopher: the name of this thinker's school may derive from the Cynosarges, a temple of Heracles where he lectured
Philosopher: followers of this thinker identified with the god Heracles as exemplifying their ideals
Philosopher: anti-hedonist founder of Cynicism who declared 'I would rather go mad than feel pleasure'
Philosopher: fictional 'Cynic epistles' in the Roman Empire were attributed to Crates of Thebes and this founder of Cynicism
Philosopher: Diogenes plucked a chicken to refute Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped,' having learned from this teacher
Philosopher: Diogenes's dog-like following of this earlier thinker may have inspired the name of Cynicism
Philosopher: could see a specific horse but not the idea of 'horsehood,' rejecting Platonic Forms
Philosopher: in Xenophon's Symposium, this thinker cast Xanthippe as 'the hardest to get along with of all the women'
Philosopher: both this thinker and Aeschines wrote lost dialogues named for Aspasia of Miletus
